statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given an integer array $ a $ of size $ n $ .

Let's call an array good if it can be obtained using the following algorithm: create an array consisting of any single integer; and then perform the following operation an arbitrary number of times: choose an element from the already existing array (let's call it $ x $ ) and add $ x $ , $ (x-1) $ , or $ (x+1) $ to the end of the array.

For example, the arrays $ [1, 2, 1] $ , $ [5] $ and $ [3, 2, 1, 4] $ are good, while $ [2, 4] $ and $ [3, 1, 2] $ are not.

Your task is to count the number of good contiguous subarrays of the array $ a $ . Two subarrays that have the same elements but are in different positions of the array $ a $ are considered different.

Input Format
The first line contains a single integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) — the number of test cases.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 3 \cdot 10^5 $ ).

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_i \le n) $ .

Additional constraint on the input: the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 3 \cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the number of good contiguous subarrays of the array $ a $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
3
1 1 3
4
3 2 3 1
1
1
8
4 5 6 5 3 2 3 1
Output
4
9
1
23

Note
In the first example, the following four subarrays are good:

- from the $ 1 $ -st to the $ 1 $ -st element;
- from the $ 1 $ -st to the $ 2 $ -nd element;
- from the $ 2 $ -nd to the $ 2 $ -nd element;
- from the $ 3 $ -rd to the $ 3 $ -rd element.

In the second example, the only subarray which is not good is the subarray from the $ 3 $ -rd element to the $ 4 $ -th element.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2011F - Good Subarray
Let's calculate the array $$$\mathit{prev}_i$$$ — the nearest element to the left of $$$i$$$ that can add the $$$i$$$-th element during the execution of the algorithm (i.e., the nearest value to the left among $$$(a_i-1)$$$, $$$a_i$$$, and $$$(a_i+1)$$$).
Note that a subarray $$$[l, r]$$$ is good if there is no index $$$j$$$ such that $$$l  \lt  j \le r$$$, and $$$\mathit{prev}_j  \lt  l$$$. It follows that for a fixed left boundary $$$l$$$, we need to find the minimum $$$j$$$ for which the above conditions hold, and then the answer can be increased by $$$(j - l)$$$, since $$$r \in [l, j)$$$ are good.
Using that fact, we can iterate over the left boundary of the segment $$$l$$$ from right to left. And maintain a stack of all indices $$$j$$$ such that $$$j  \gt  l$$$ and $$$\mathit{prev}_j  \lt  l$$$. If the value at the top of the stack no longer satisfies this condition when moving to the next value of $$$l$$$, it can be removed (since the condition doesn't hold for all next $$$l$$$ either). After that, the top of the stack contains the minimum $$$j$$$, that allows us to update the answer (if the stack is empty, any right boundary is good). After that, it is sufficient to push the index $$$l$$$ onto the stack and move to the next value.
Thus, we have obtained a solution that works in $$$O(n)$$$.

pos

a

(
